speaker: interviewer
text: With Rabbi Jacob Jungreis, February 28, 1995, Brooklyn, New York, interviewer Ricky Halperin. So the day of your transport arrives.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day of our transport arrives, and they take us evening time to the rail station. It was a rainy night. And we-- we were, at least, I would say, at least 1,000 people, possibly more, out in an open field. And they told us that we'd have to sleep this night in pup tents, like Boy Scouts use, on the floor in the mud. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's a moment of this night that I will never forget because for me, emotionally, it was devastating. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My younger sister, Esther, had fever that night. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my dad and mom went around the entire camp to see if someone would have an aspirin. And no one had an aspirin. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I remember my father coming back from searching around the entire camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I saw that expression on his face. That expression of absolute despair and pain and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] utter suffering, I will never forget in my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That time I felt, I, like, lost it. You know, to us, parents, they're our protectors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, if I have my father there or my mother there, then-- then I'm protected. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was something I couldn't speak about, of course. You can't discuss this with your parents, but that was the feeling, I had. If my father is helpless, and he came with that mood, that feeling of despair, he can not get that aspirin for a sick child. He was crying all night, with tears. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was probably one of the worst moments for me in the entire period of the Shoah. That's when I felt I lost it. I totally lost it. I-- the-- the parents can not help? And that's a horrible feeling. I think for a child, it-- it was devastating.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the night. Thank god she got through, eventually. And then, in the morning, the rain stopped. And they made us walk, which was about two blocks away, to the railroad station, and there, already, there were some Germans, too. Mainly the Gentiles, Germans. They were screaming, [NON-ENGLISH], means fast, fast, fast. And from that moment on, until we were liberated, that was that word that I remember the most, [NON-ENGLISH], fast. Mach schnell. Mach schnell. Make it fast. And everything had to be done fast. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they put us on this train, packed it quite full, and closed the door. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As a kid yet, I loved a train ride, always, but I realized this is not one of those train rides. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You remember little moments because really, life is made up of little things, not big things. I remember the train started to move. And after a while, I had to urinate. I was very embarrassed. So, a kid can do it better than an adult, I imagine, to hold it in. And then it became night, so my father and mother said, you know what, there's a little opening on top of the wagon, of this cattle car, we'll lift you up and try to do it that way. It didn't work. Of course, that night I wetted myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, the train arrives to Budapest. They opened the door of our wagon. And, Jungreis? Jungreis. You-- the family-- you get off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were scared. We thought because he's the rabbi, that they are taking off to kill him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they took us away. And they took us into the ghetto of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was with grandparents, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. They-- they lived in other cities. We had, in 1944, 87 Rabbi Jungreises in Hungary. We were the largest rabbinical family. We are still the largest rabbinical family that exists in the world. And everyone was a rabbi in a city or a village, in a different city, and in a different village. And everybody was a practicing rabbi. We did not have that notion that they were to get a rabbinical degree, and to call yourself a rabbi and not practice it. That was not the custom at all. And they took us off this train and into the Budapest ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And once we got in there, we heard some good news-- that they are assembling a group of people, and this is the way they told us the story, who are going to be sent to Palestine through Spain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's amazing during that the [? Shoah ?] the dictators were friends of the Jews. The Spanish dictator at that time was-- I forgot his name. He was the dictator until lately. And they said that we will go through Spain to Palestine. What really happened was, and the reason they took us off this train, that I had an aunt, Elsa Jungreis, of blessed memory-- my father's sister. And she worked in the office of a gentleman by the name of Kastner, [NON-ENGLISH], of blessed memory. He was the President of the Hungarian Zionist movement. And it seems that he made a deal with this Eichmann, [SPEAKING HEBREW] may his name be erased, that he would let 1,850 Jews out of Hungary for $5 million. As a matter of fact, he offered to let all the Hungarian Jews free for money and trucks and coffee, for all types of goodies. But this deal was made definite, and as my aunt told me the story that Kastner came in one day to the office of the Zionist movement. And he said he can save now 1,850 Jews and please give me names. Whoever has names, put it on the list. He also said, and I want to emphasize this specifically, that he wants to save-- this is a Talmudic expression called [SPEAKING HEBREW] which refers to the students and the scholars. He wanted to save as many of the sages of Hungary, as possible. And that's why they put on this list also the very holy Rabbi of Satu Mare of blessed memory, and Rabbi Steif, who was the head of the [INAUDIBLE] of Hungary, and also many Rabbis. I don't remember all their names. I remember one other very famous rabbi at the time, was name of-- his name was Rabbi Strasser. He was a gentleman who was 87 years old at the time-- a tall gentleman, a beautiful gentlemen. He was the Rabbi of Debrecen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the last Rabbi of Debrecen, and many others. My aunt put all the Rabbi Jungreises on this list, as many as she could, and of course her parents-- My grandfather was the Rabbi of [HUNGARIAN]-- and all the uncles and cousins, but it was too late. They were already deported. The only one who was still in Hungary were us, because we were in this large city, and the-- the mood in the large cities was more civilized, and always the large cities were the last ones to be deported. But everyone else was already gone. It was too late. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he also assembled, Kastner, 300 boys-- Jewish boys and girls, teenagers between the age of about 16 to 19, 20. Not Hungarians-- Lithuanians who smuggled themselves into Hungary, who saw their parents being killed in front of their own eyes. And Lithuania, of course, is known for scholarship. These boys were Talmudic geniuses. And the girls are also very well-learned. The Lithuanian community, more than any other community, emphasized education-- very educated. And Kastner put them on this group because they would be shot if found they were illegal in Hungary. These boys and girls did not believe after this. They were like atheists. And the other people did not want to be too friendly with them. My family became very friendly with them, and that's where our families career, which is, to this day, of working with Jews that are unaffiliated, or very removed, started. And we realized that when you see a Jew who's not observant, and you give them a hug and a kiss, he will respond. And the train was assembled, and that's when we started to leave Budapest. We also were in cattle cars, but the doors are open. It was a slow-moving train, and it was not packed. We had about 30 people to a car, and the-- many people sat at the door with their feet dangling, as we took this trip. And we came to Vienna. And then we stopped in an Austrian town called Linz. In Linz, they asked everyone to get off, and they took us into the city, or the outskirts of the city somewhere. They said we have to have a shower. And this was our first face-to-face meeting with the realities. This shower in Linz was actually a gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a shower, but you could-- it was a gas chamber, but you could also take a shower. We knew it was a gas chamber because we saw cases of poison gas clearly, with the crossed bones that it can kill you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they said that first the woman will take the showers. And prior to taking showers, it seems they shaved every lady. And there was a tremendous screaming emanating from this building. And the man there told me, listen, you are a boy. Nothing will happen. Run over. See what's happening. The men were very upset. I ran over. I saw that everyone is being shaved. And a German soldier saw me and gave me-- the first time I got a very painful hit with a rifle butt on my back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we went to take the showers, the men. What I remember was, hurtful. They disinfected us, and that solution itched and burned a lot, but it was a very burning solution. And we took the showers and went back to our train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who guarded these trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: These trains were guarded by Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their overall treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The overall treatment was not bad, was not bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were treated, I would say-- I mean, at that time we thought it was horrible. But in the context of the situation, they treated us with deference. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this trip from Budapest to Bergen-Belsen took over a week. And the train pulled into a city not far from Bergen-Belsen called Celle. Celle was the nearest railroad station. Bergen-Belsen is in North Germany. The nearest city's Celle, and the city that's more prominent further away is Hanover. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At Celle, they asked everyone to get off the train, take out their luggage, leave it there, and the luggage would be collected by trucks, and we should walk. It was a terribly long walk, almost endless. Thank God it was springtime, summer. It was a four-hour walk to Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We come to Bergen-Belsen and see barbed wires all around. And they gave us two barracks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two sections. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone asked, well, this is not Spain, you know? We are supposed to go to Spain and to Palestine. So we took it, you know, that we were misled. But some of the leaders of the group said not to worry. Eventually, you know, this is just a stop. We'll get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everyone was assigned to a barracks. The barracks had three-tiered beds. And we, the children-- we slept with my mother on one narrow bed. How we fit, I can't understand to this day. On the third level of this three-tiered bed, was a family, a lady with seven children and some of them babies. And every night, they wetted themselves. And this came down on our faces. So, after a while, it was arranged that I went to sleep with my father in the other barrack. Men and women slept in separate barracks. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This Bergen-Belsen was in middle of a forest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing that came to our attention was this is the middle of a German forest. Where are the birds? There isn't one bird to be seen anywhere-- not a chirp.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I speak occasionally about these things, and I always say it's my unscientific conclusion that birds like to go only to nice places. There wasn't a bird in Bergen-Belsen. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] We were waiting, and with every day that passed, we became more and more hopeless. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did not kill us outright. They did not give us special garments like the other prisoners had. We were adjacent to the Polish camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The main tasks was primarily cleaning, sanitation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] working to prepare some food, and to bring the food in. There was very little food. Very little. My dad gave his rations to the children. He ate only on the Shabbat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He once even fainted from hunger. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The currency of the concentration camp was cigarettes. With cigarettes, you could buy everything. They gave us a portion of cigarettes, and everyone who never smoked in his life said he's a smoker, like my father. And he kept these cigarettes to see if he could exchange it for something else. There were people who gave up their daily rations of food for a smoke. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Nighttime was a time when people made announcements. About 12 at night, that's when everyone was in bed, and people make an announcement. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most-- and also we tried to, like, speak to each other, you know, to have a semblance of civility and humanity. There were people who, at night, were like professional storytellers who would get up. And most stories that people said were like hindsight. There were a lot of people with us who just came back from Western Europe to Hungary, because they didn't think this would happen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People told us all types of stories how if they would have known, they wouldn't have to be here. I remember one announcement, which I'll never forget, because that really says it all, how it was there. We had the richest Hungarian Jew in our midst. He was like the President of the Hungarian community. His name was Freudiger, an older gentlemen. Somehow, he smuggled a can of sardines into Bergen-Belsen, and he opened it. He turned around for one second, and it was gone. That night, he makes an announcement. And as you know, this was the symbol, this Mr. Freudiger, the symbol of aristocracy, of civility, of dignity. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, who stole my can of sardines? I'm asking it. I'm asking him to give it back to me, because I've spit into it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When people heard that, they said, God, we really are in the gutter. If a man like Freudiger can make such a statement, then we are in the gutter. That really depressed everybody. It, like, said it all. It reflected our true state of being. We are in the gutter. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a kid, we did not do slave labor. We didn't work. People had time, all day gossip and talking. I listened in. I had very big ears, and I listened to everything. I took a lot of stuff to heart. And there was a tremendous amount of self-blame. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Number one, they blamed the rabbis, the Hasidic rabbis, who were anti-Zionist, who discouraged people from going on aliyah to settle in Israel. If not for them, then we would be in Israel, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In retrospect, of course, I speak on this subject, and I don't blame anyone for this, because number one, whoever wanted to go, went and went to the rabbi and said, I'm leaving. Give me a blessing. They did not ask. Those who asked, wanted to hear they should not go. But that's not even the issue. The issue is in today's terms, when we go to Israel today, and we are there in 10 hours, and the moment we get there, we call up. When someone went to Israel before the Second World War, chances were he never saw his family ever again. Chances were he could never speak to them ever again, and it took two months for a letter to arrive. And we had very close-knit families. It was very hard to ask a young boy or girl to leave his family and never to-- never ever to see them again. In the context of the-- of those times, I believe our people acted correctly. It's difficult to go away from your father and mother, knowing that you will never, ever see them again. In fact, when my uncle came to America before the war, his mother told him, my son, I know I will never see you again. That was the good-bye. But there was a lot of self-recrimination. And over there, I learned the whole concept of Zionism. I had never heard of that before. I heard about Zionist once in my home, when somebody told me I want to teach you a secret-- one of these boys who was my home-- and he taught me the first Hebrew song, called [SPEAKING HEBREW], which is my favorite song to this day. But he said, don't tell your parents I taught you this. Zionism was not that much part of life, but it became the-- the number one topic in Bergen-Belsen. And it's the first time I heard of a date in Jewish history, I had never heard of before, and that date is November 2, 1917-- the Balfour Declaration. I never heard of that. I learned about it in Bergen-Belsen. And there was his holy sage, the Rabbi of Debrecen-- the true Rabbi of Debrecen-- who every day, all day, went around saying that God is punishing us because we neglected Israel. We neglected [SPEAKING HEBREW]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no question about that. If we ever get out, there's only one place to go to. It's Israel. And everyone there was a Zionist, even the sages who people think they were not, there was no doubt over there that they were. You know, in a trench, everybody believes in God. And in Bergen-Belsen, everybody was a Zionist. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There came the holidays, the high holidays-- Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. We had only the one [SPEAKING HEBREW], or prayer book, for the high holidays for 1,850 people. And the rabbis of Bergen-Belsen unanimously decided that the whole camp should learn one prayer by heart, that when we pray, at least this one prayer everybody should say in unison. And this prayer was called [SPEAKING HEBREW], which is part of the high holidays service. It means, oh, God who examines the innermost recesses of the heart. And this prayer, [SPEAKING HEBREW], was learned by heart. For weeks, we learned it, one from another, because this was the decision-- the unanimous decision-- of the rabbis. And the implication, also, was that we have no words. Because we have no prayer books, we cannot speak. So God has to look into our hearts, instead of listening to prayers. Also, we found out that the Polish camp next to us has a shofar. And had no-- they were like slaves. They had no privileges at all. We made a deal with them. We collected 200 cigarettes to obtain the shofar from the Polish camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then came the high holidays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Holy Rabbi of Satu Mare, he prayed. He had that only prayer book. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we came to that segment of [SPEAKING HEBREW], God, look into our hearts, and you will understand our troubles, everybody cried. And everybody said it with great fervor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then came the blowing of the shofar. And the holy Rabbi of Satu Mare, he blew the shofar. I stood at the window of the barracks where we prayed. I remember it like today. I don't think it was my imagination, but those walls of those barracks shook. They really trembled. It was so charged, the atmosphere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I look out the window from these barracks, and across I see a big truck being pushed-- and I counted-- by about 19 Polish Jews. This was the German way of torturing people-- to load up an empty truck with junk, make people take it someplace, empty it, and then take it back. And they heard the shofar. They stopped for a second. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then the Ukrainian guards and some of the kapos and the German guards who were there started to be beat them mercilessly. I say to this day, in my sermons, that the Jewish nation such-- has such a huge
